coredata.dc:title: Electrochemically reduced TiO2 photoanode coupled with oxygen vacancy-rich carbon quantum dots for synergistically improving photoelectrochemical performance
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Tingsheng
Li, Lei
Li, Jinhua
Wang, Jiachen
Bai, Jing
Xia, Ligang
Xu, Qunjie
Zhou, Baoxue
coredata.dc:description: Severe bulk charge recombination, sluggish oxygen evolution reaction (OER) kinetics and poor visible light harvesting are still the technical bottlenecks of famous TiO2 photoanode for photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting. Here, a novel CQDs/E-TiO2 photoanode was designed based on the accurately electrochemical reduction of TiO2 (E-TiO2, Ti4+ + e− → Ti3+) and further modification of oxygen vacancy (OV)-rich carbon quantum dots (CQDs) for synergistically improving PEC performance. The electrochemical reduction creates moderate OV in TiO2, which increase the majority carrier density and provide photoinduced charge traps for sharply increasing the bulk charge separation efficiency (η bulk). The CQDs modification dramatically improves the surface charge transfer efficiency (η surface) by serving as oxygen evolution catalysts (OECs), because the abundant OV in CQDs greatly promote the interfacial OER kinetics. Additionally, the visible light harvesting of TiO2 is significantly improved after CQDs modification. Near-complete bulk charge separation (η bulk = 94.7%) is achieved for E-TiO2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE (VRHE), which is 4.0 times higher than that of TiO2. The CQDs/E-TiO2 shows the η surface of 56.0% at 0.40 VRHE, which is 7.2 times higher than E-TiO2. Therefore, the CQDs/E-TiO2 exhibits remarkable photocurrent densities of 1.50 mA cm−2 at 0.60 VRHE and 2.55 mA cm−2 at 1.23 VRHE, which are 27.0 and 10.0 times higher than TiO2, 3.5 and 1.5 times higher than E-TiO2, respectively.

head.ce:title: Electrochemically reduced TiO2 photoanode coupled with oxygen vacancy-rich carbon quantum dots for synergistically improving photoelectrochemical performance

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Severe bulk charge recombination, sluggish oxygen evolution reaction (OER) kinetics and poor visible light harvesting are still the technical bottlenecks of famous TiO2 photoanode for photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting. Here, a novel CQDs/E-TiO2 photoanode was designed based on the accurately electrochemical reduction of TiO2 (E-TiO2, Ti4+ + e− → Ti3+) and further modification of oxygen vacancy (OV)-rich carbon quantum dots (CQDs) for synergistically improving PEC performance. The electrochemical reduction creates moderate OV in TiO2, which increase the majority carrier density and provide photoinduced charge traps for sharply increasing the bulk charge separation efficiency (η bulk). The CQDs modification dramatically improves the surface charge transfer efficiency (η surface) by serving as oxygen evolution catalysts (OECs), because the abundant OV in CQDs greatly promote the interfacial OER kinetics. Additionally, the visible light harvesting of TiO2 is significantly improved after CQDs modification. Near-complete bulk charge separation (η bulk = 94.7%) is achieved for E-TiO2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE (VRHE), which is 4.0 times higher than that of TiO2. The CQDs/E-TiO2 shows the η surface of 56.0% at 0.40 VRHE, which is 7.2 times higher than E-TiO2. Therefore, the CQDs/E-TiO2 exhibits remarkable photocurrent densities of 1.50 mA cm−2 at 0.60 VRHE and 2.55 mA cm−2 at 1.23 VRHE, which are 27.0 and 10.0 times higher than TiO2, 3.5 and 1.5 times higher than E-TiO2, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para:
Since the first time Honda and Fujishima used TiO2 as the photoanode for hydrogen generation by Photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting in 1972 [1], the photoanode materials that determine the rate-limiting step of a PEC device due to the sluggish oxygen evolution reaction (OER) kinetics [2–7], have been intensively studied to improve the solar to hydrogen (STH) conversion efficiency, aiming at mitigating energy crisisandenvironmental emission issues [2–5,8–12]. The water oxidation photocurrent density (J H2O) can be expressed as [2,4,13]:
(1)
J
H
2
O
=
J
abs
×
η
bulk
×
η
surface
where J abs is the photocurrent density calculated by assuming that the absorbed photons are completely converted to current density; η bulk is the bulk charge separation efficiency that represents the yield of the photogenerated holes reaching the electrode surface, and η surface is the surface charge transfer efficiency that represents the yield of the surface holes injected into theelectrolyte. TiO2 as a promising photoanode candidate has many advantages, such as facile synthesis, low cost, richreserves, excellent chemical stability, eco-friendliness, favorable band-edge positions [3,4,11,14–16]. However, TiO2 suffers from poor visible light harvesting (low J abs), severe bulk charge recombination (low η bulk) and sluggish OER kinetics (low η surface), seriously blocking the PEC activity [3,4,9,11]. Therefore, the PEC activity of TiO2 can be significantly improved by increasing the J abs , η bulk and η surface.

To this end, many efforts have been made, such as doping [17,18], introduction of oxygenvacancies (OV) [15,16,19–21], quantum dots sensitization [4,19,22], heterojunction construction [3,4,10,23,24] and surface modification of OER catalysts (OECs) [5,25,26], etc. The η bulk can be efficiently improved by inducing OV into TiO2, because the OV function as shallow donors that increase the majority carrier density and provide photoinduced charge traps for promoting bulk charge separation [8,27,28]. However, excessive OV will in turn degrade the PEC performance of TiO2 for the fact that a large number of Ti3+ ions act as recombination centers of electron-hole pairs [28]. Meanwhile, the presence of OV can promote the surface OER processes, but the OV can also create surface trap states that result in interfacial charge recombination [29]. Therefore, tuning the OV density in TiO2 for balancing the advantageous effects on bulk charge separation and surface reaction and the disadvantageous effects of bulk and interfacial charge recombination is crucial to the water oxidation performance. However, the OV created by the commonmethods, such as hydrogenation [15,21,30], hydrogen plasma treatment [20] or chemical reduction [16,19] cannot be precisely controlled. Additionally, the above methods are usually dangerous and high cost, seriouslylimitingthe practicalapplications. Since the OV can be induced by reducing Ti4+ to Ti3+ (Ti4+ + e− → Ti3+), the amount of Ti3+ can be accurately controlled by adjusting the charge quantity provided by an electrochemical work station. Therefore, the moderate OV should be accurately introduced into TiO2 by a simple, safe and low-cost electrochemical treatment, which was rarely reported for TiO2.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as a promising photoelectricmaterial have inspired extensive attention due to many advantages, such as eco-friendiness, low cost, simple synthesis, chemicalinertness and up-conversion Photoluminescence (PL) properties [2,4,31–33]. CQDs as visible light sensitizers can significantly improve the visible light harvesting range, thereby directly increasing the J abs [2,4]. CQDs can also form heterojunctions with some metal oxide semiconductors, which generates more holes reaching the electrode surface, such as the reported CQDs/Fe2O3 [34], CQDs/BiVO4 [2], CQDs/WO3 [35], CQDs/ZnO [36] and CQDs/TiO2 [4,22,37], etc. Most of all, we found that the OV-rich CQDs could serve as the role of OECs, because the OV in CQDs promote the hole transfer, which efficiently reduce the energy barrier of OER, thereby improving the η surface [2,4]. The role and mechanism of OV-rich CQDs as OECs were rarely reported before.

Based on the above considerations, a novel CQDs/E-TiO2 photoanode is prepared by a simple electrochemical reduction of TiO2 (E-TiO2) and further modification of OV-rich CQDs. The electrochemical reduction accurately induces moderate OV into TiO2, sharply increasing the η bulk. Meanwhile, the OV-rich CQDs simultaneously serve as the roles of OECs and visible light sensitizers, significantly improving the η surface and J abs, respectively. It is the synergistic effect of electrochemically induced OV in TiO2 and OV-rich CQDs that dramatically improves the PEC performance. Compared with TiO2, a 10-fold improvement of the photocurrent density (2.55 mA cm−2) is achieved for CQDs/E-TiO2 at 1.23 vs. RHE (VRHE), along with a remarkably negative shift of onset potential.

All reagents were analytical grade and were used directly without further purification. First, the fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) coated glasses (25 mm × 50 mm, 7 Ω/cm2) were ultrasonically washed by acetone, ethanol and deionized water for 30 min, respectively. The TiO2 films were synthesized according to a reported hydrothermal method [3]. Typically, 0.6 mL Tetrabutyltitanate (TBOT) was added dropwise into 30 mL mixed solution of equal volume of deionized water and HCl (wt%: 36%~38%) under continuous agitation until the solution became clear. The solution was then transferred into 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, and the FTO substrate was angled against the wall of Teflon vessel with the FTO side facing down, which was heated to 150 ℃ for 12 h. After cooling down, the TiO2 film was rinsed with deionized water and calcined at 450 ℃ for 1 h. Then, the TiO2 film was immersed in 1 M NaOH electrolyte (pH 13.6) with a platinum wire and an Ag/AgCl electrode as the counter and reference electrodes, respectively. A reduced bias of −2.0 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) was applied for TiO2 electrode for 20 s, followed by washing with deionized water and drying at 50 °C overnight to obtain the E-TiO2. To optimize the properties of E-TiO2, the different potentials and time for electrochemical reduction of TiO2 films were conducted.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQDs/TiO2 and CQDs/E-TiO2 films
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs were synthesized through a one-step alkali-assisted ultrasonic chemical method [2]. In a typical synthesis, 9 g of glucose was dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water. Then 50 mL of NaOH solution (1 M) was added into the glucose solution, followed by ultrasonic treatment for 6 hat room temperature. The obtained solution was adjusted to pH = 7 with HCl, and then a equal volume of ethanol was added into the solution under agitation for 1 h. For the preparation of CQDs/TiO2 and CQDs/E-TiO2 films, 0.5 mL of the above CQDs solution was dipped on the surface of TiO2 or E-TiO2 with a transfer liquid gun, followed by spin-coating at 1000 rpm for 10 s, and then dried in air at 100 °C for 30 min on a hot plate. The procedure was repeated for two times.

The morphology and composition were examined by field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, Ultra Plus, Zeiss, Germany) and high-resolution transmission electronic microscopy (HRTEM; Tecnai G2 F30 S-TWIN, FEI, USA) with Energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS). The crystalline structures were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD; AXS-8 Advance, Bruker, Germany) with a Cu Kα (λ = 0.15406 nm) source. The light absorption spectra were recorded with a UV–vis spectrophotometer (TU1900, Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co, China). The surface elemental compositions and states were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, AXIS Ultra DLD, Kratos, Japan) with an Al Kα source (hν = 1486.6 eV). The EPR spectra were measured by a Bruker Electron Paramagnetic Resonance Spectrometer (EPR 300E). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded using a Nicolet iS5 FTIR Spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific) scanning from 4000–400 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1.

The PEC measurements were carried out on an electrochemical workstation (CHI 660D, CH Instruments Inc., USA) using a three-electrode system with a photoanode, a platinum foil and a saturatedAg/AgCl electrode as the working electrode, counter electrode and the reference electrode, respectively, in 0.2 M Na2SO4 electrolyte (pH = 7) under solar illumination intensity of 100 mW cm−2 provided by a 350-W xenon lamp with AM 1.5G (Shanghai Hualun Bulb Factory). The measured potentials versus Ag/AgCl electrode were converted to the Reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) scale according to the Nernst formula:
(2)
E
RHE
=
E
Ag / AgCl
+ 0.0591 × pH + 0.1976
where E RHE represents the converted potential vs. RHE. E Ag/AgCl is the obtained potential vs. Ag/AgCl. All the experiments were performed at ambient temperature. The scan rate of the photocurrent-potential curve (J-V) was 50 mV/s. The incident photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) was measured at 1.23 VRHE using a xenon arc lamp (500 W, Zolix, China) equipped with a monochromator. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed at 1.23 VRHE with amplitude perturbations of 5 mV and a frequency range from 1 Hz to 10 kHz under AM 1.5G simulated solar light (100 mWcm−2). Mott-Schottky (M-S) measurements were performed with the frequency of 1 kHz under dark. Intensity-modulated photocurrent and photovoltage spectroscopies (IMPS/IMVS) were performed using an electrochemical workstation (Zennium; effect-Elektrik, Germany) equiped with an illumination intensity-modulated photospectroscopy (CIMPS, PP211) and a white-light lamp (WLC02; Zahner-Elektrik) that allowed the superimposition of sinusoidal modulation (~10 %) on DC illumination. The modulation frequency of 10 mHz-1 kHz, the illumination intensity of 40 mW cm−2 and the potential of 1.23 VRHE were set. Thetransient photocurrent/photovoltage (TPC/TPV) were recorded under the illumination intensity of 60 mW cm−2, using a laser pulse (10 Hz, NT342A-10, EKSPLA) and a white-light lamp (WLC02; Zahner-Elektrik) equiped with an digital oscilloscope (Agilent, DSO-X2024A).

The crystal structures of the electrodes were first characterized by XRD. The XRD patterns of the TiO2 films with different load of CQDs are shown in Fig. S1. Except for the SnO2 peaks appearing at 26.59°, 33.87°, 37.72°, 51.83°, 61.72° and 65.89° originating from the FTO substrate (PDF#46-1088), all other diffraction peaks appearing at 36.08°, 41.24°, 54.32°, 62.76° and 69.80° in the XRD patterns for CQDs/TiO2 are well assigned to the rutile TiO2 (PDF#75-1748), which indicates the CQDs signal can hardly be detected by XRD. The The completely identical XRD patterns of TiO2 and E-TiO2 imply that the electrochemical reductionhasnoeffectonthecrystal-phase of TiO2 (Fig. S2). The morphology and composition of TiO2, E-TiO2 and CQDs/E-TiO2 were analyzed by SEM and EDS. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the TiO2 nanorod arrays are vertically grown on the FTO substrate, with the average thickness of 2.7 μm. After electrochemical reduction or modification of CQDs, no obvious morphology and thickness change can be observed (Fig. 1b and 1c). The EDS and elemental mapping of TiO2, E-TiO2 and CQDs/E-TiO2 are exhibited in Fig. S3-S5. Without detecting any carbon signal for TiO2 and E-TiO2, a relatively high carbon content (22.64 at%) is detected for CQDs/E-TiO2, indicating the successful decoration of CQDs on E-TiO2. The pure CQDs solution was characterized by TEM, from which it can be observed that the CQDs with the mean size of 3.0 nm are uniformly distributed (Fig. 1d). The HRTEM image of CQDs shows the lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) facet ofgraphite [38]. The typical lattice fringe spacing of 0.324 nm can be observed from the HRTEM of CQDs/E-TiO2, corresponding to the (110) crystal plane of the rutile TiO2 (Fig. 1e–1 h) [15,26,39,40]. The lattice spacings of 0.21 nm and 0.32 nm can be also observed, corresponding to the (100) and (002) facets ofgraphite, respectively [2,38]. The elemental mapping of CQDs/E-TiO2 shown in Fig. 1i–1 l gives three elements of Ti, O and C, in which the C element is evenly distributed with the content of 25.8 at% (Fig. S6), showing that CQDs were uniformly modified on the surface of E-TiO2.

As shown in Fig. 2 a, the pristine TiO2 shows very low PEC activity, with the photocurrent density of only 0.256 mA cm−2 at 1.23 VRHE. To improve the PEC activity of TiO2, the Ov were introduced into TiO2 by a simple electrochemical reduction method. The electrochemical reduction at −2.0 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) in 1.0 M NaOH for 20 s was found to be the optimized condition (Fig. S7). The optimal E-TiO2 shows a high photocurrent density of 1.74 mA cm−2 at 1.23 VRHE, which is 6.8 times higher than the pristine TiO2. The loading amount of CQDs for PEC property was optimized in Fig. S8, in which the optimal CQDs/TiO2 shows obviously improved photocurrent density (0.461 mA cm−2 at 1.23 VRHE) and negative shift of onset potential in comparison to TiO2. Loading CQDs on E-TiO2, a remarkable improvement of photocurrent density and a dramatically negative shift of onset potential are observed for the obtained CQDs/E-TiO2. The CQDs/E-TiO2 shows remarkable photocurrent densities of 1.50 mA cm−2 at 0.6 VRHE and 2.55 mA cm−2 at 1.23 VRHE, which are 27.0 and 10.0 times higher than TiO2, 3.5 and 1.5 times higher than E-TiO2, respectively. The enlarged chopped J-V curves of the samples at low potential range (0.3–0.9 VRHE) are shown in Fig. 2b. The TiO2 and E-TiO2 show sharp transient spikes upon removing the lightbarrier, which is ascribed to the fact that the aggregated holes recombine with the electrons at the photoanode surface [2–4]. As the potential rises, the transient spikes are obviously attenuated, which is because the recombination of electron-hole pairs is suppressed at higher potentials. After modification of CQDs, no transient spike can be observed for the obtained CQDs/E-TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2, suggesting CQDs efficiently suppress the recombination of electron-hole pairs at the electrode surface. Fig. 2c shows the chopped photocurrent-time responses of the above samples at 1.23 VRHE, which agree well with the data in Fig. 2a.

The photoelectric conversion capacities of the photoanodes were evaluated by the ABPE and IPCE measurements. Supposing 100% of Faradaic efficiency, the ABPE can be calculated by the formula [3]:
(3)
A B P E
%
=
J
1.23 - V
P
× 100
where J is the photocurrent density (mA cm−2), V is the applied bias (VRHE) and P is the incident illumination power density (100 mW cm−2). As shown in Fig. 2d, the maximum ABPE of TiO2 is only 0.042 % at 0.916 VRHE, but the maximum ABPE of E-TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 are increased to 0.355% at 0.834 VRHE and 0.126% at 0.725 VRHE, respectively. A maximum ABPE of 0.997 % at a low potential of 0.514 VRHE is observed for CQDs/E-TiO2, which is 23.7, 2.8 and 7.9 times higher than TiO2, E-TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2, respectively. The IPCE is calculated by the formula [3]:
(4)
I P C E
%
=
1240 J
λ
λ P
λ
× 100
where J(λ), λ and P(λ) are the measured photocurrent density at specific wavelength, the wavelength of incident light and the measured light power density at that wavelength, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2e, the maximum IPCE value of CQDs/E-TiO2 reaches up to 77.8% at the wavelength of 390 nm, which is much higher than that of TiO2 (9.87%), CQDs/TiO2 (18.7%) and E-TiO2 (56.4%). The pristine TiO2 shows no photocurrent response at the wavelength below 360 nm and above 420 nm. With modification of CQDs, the photocurrent response covers the whole measured wavelength range (300–600 nm).

The long-term PEC stability as well as water splitting performance of CQDs/E-TiO2 was measured at 1.23 VRHE under illumination of 100 mW cm−2 (Fig. 2f). The CQDs/E-TiO2 shows a photocurrent density of 2.58 mA cm−2 without obvious decay after 2 h of operation, suggesting the good PEC stability. The CQDs/E-TiO2 after PEC stability test was characterized by the XPS and HTEM, disclosing that the substantially unchanged OV content and the CQDs steadily attached on TiO2 surface account for the good stability (Fig. S9). The PEC water splitting performance of CQDs/E-TiO2 was assessed by the gas evolution rate and Faradaic efficiency (ξ). The average H2 and O2 evolution rates of CQDs/E-TiO2 are 44.1 and 21.1 μmol cm−2h−1, respectively. The ξ can be calculated by the formula [2,3]:
(5)
ξ =
α F n
∫
t 1
t 2
J d t
× 100 %
where α is 2 for H2 and 4 for O2; F is the Faraday constant 96,485 C mol−1); n is the amount of H2 or O2 (mol); J is the photocurrent (A); t 1 and t 2 are the start and end time (s), respectively. The ξ of H2 and O2 evolution for CQDs/E-TiO2 are ~90%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Origin of the improved PEC activity

ce:section.ce:para:
Because the visible light harvesting has a positive relationwith the J abs that directly influences the PEC activity, the optical properties of the photoanodes were investigated by UV-vis absorption spectra. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the E-TiO2 shows almost overlapped UV-Vis spectrum with the pristine TiO2, but with a little improvement of visble light harvesting, which reveals that the influence of light absorption induced by electrochemical reduction on PEC conversion enhancement is negligible. However, after modification of CQDs on TiO2, the visible light as well as UV light is greatly improved with the increased loading amount of CQDs (Fig. S10). The E g of the photoanodes can be calculated by the eq. [4]:
(6)
α h v = A
hv -
E g
2
whereα,h, v, A and E g are the absorption coefficient, Planck's constant, light frequency,proportionality constant and band gap energy, respectively. The E g values of TiO2, E-TiO2 are calculated to be 2.99 and 2.98 eV, respectively. The results indicate that the enhanced light harvesting induced by CQDs contributes to the enhancement of PEC conversion.

To investigate the effects of electrochemical reduction and CQDs on the structures of TiO2, the XPS and EPR spectra were further performed. As shown in Fig. 3b, the peaks centered at the binding energies (BE) of 458.56 eV and 464.30 eV are assigned to Ti 2p3/2and Ti 2p1/2, respectively, coinciding with Ti4+ values in TiO2 lattice [3,4,19]. The Ti 2p peaks of E-TiO2 are shifted to lower BE in comparison to TiO2, which is due to partial reduction of Ti4+ to Ti3+ ions [8,16,19,28]. Compared with TiO2, the shift to higher BE for the Ti 2p peaks of CQDs/TiO2 suggests the strong interaction between the TiO2 and CQDs, which is possibly ascribed to the formation of Ti–O–C bond [4,41,42]. As shown in Fig. 3c, the O 1 s spectra of the TiO2 are all deconvoluted into three peaks centered at 529.6 eV, 530.6 eV and 531.8 eV, corresponding to the O2− species in the lattice (OL), OV and the surface absorbed OH group (OOH), respectively [43–45]. Compared with TiO2, the O 1 s peaks of E-TiO2 are slightly shifted to lower BE, suggesting that the electrons transfer from the Conduction band (CB) to the neighboring OV states [16,19]. Additionally, the O 1 s peaks of CQDs/TiO2 display a slight shift toward higher BE, probably because of the coupling between Ti–O and C [4]. The OV content in the pristine TiO2 is only 5.6%, while the OV content in E-TiO2 is 23.0%, suggesting the efficient introduction of OV by electrochemical redution. Note that the OV content in CQDs/TiO2 is 14.0%, which is much higher than that in TiO2, indicating the existence of OV in CQDs [2]. Compared with TiO2 and E-TiO2, the occupancy ratios of OOH in CQDs/TiO2 and CQDs/E-TiO2 are greatly boosted, which might be the result of the C−O or C−OH groups from CQDs [4]. Loading CQDs on E-TiO2, the OV content is further increased to 31.1%. As shown in Fig. S11, three peaks located at 284.79, 286.30, and 288.16 eV are deconvoluted from the C 1s spectrum of CQDs/E-TiO2, corresponding to the C–C, C–O, and O=C–O groups, respectively, indicating the successful load of CQDson E-TiO2 [2,4].

To further characterize the OV, the EPR was further conducted. As shown in Fig. 3d, an obvious EPR signal at g = 2.003 appears in TiO2, which is the typical signal of OV, suggesting the existence of OV in TiO2 [16,19,46]. Compared with TiO2, the intensity of EPR signal for E-TiO2 shows a sharp increase, which indicates that more OV were induced into TiO2 by electrochemical reduction.In addition, a noticeable EPR signal observed for CQDs implies the nature of OV-rich CQDs, which may explain the role of OECs [2]. The EPR results agree well with the XPS analysis. The annealingtreatment of E-TiO2 in air results in a sharp drop of the photocurrent density (Fig. S12). This is a result of OV loss, which is evidenced by the high-resolution O 1s spectrum (Fig. S13) and EPR spectrum (Fig. S14) of the obtained air-annealed E-TiO2. The results indicate that the OV have a determined effect on the PEC property of the photoanodes. The relative content of OL, OV and OOH for the samples is summarized in Table S1.

The FTIR spectra of CQDs, TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 were measured to confirm the oxygen-containing functional groups in CQDs and to study the interaction between TiO2 and CQDs in the CQDs/TiO2 composite. As shown in Fig. 4 , the peaks at ~ 3434 and 1638 cm−1 in CQDs, TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 are attributed to the stretching andbending vibrationsof surface hydroxyl (O-H) groups, respectively [47–49]. The characteristic absorption bands of O-H at ~ 3431 cm−1, the stretching vibration bands of C–O at 1071 cm−1, and the stretching vibration bands of C=O at 1593 cm−1 indicate the presence of carboxylic acid and other oxygen-containing functional groups in CQDs [50,51]. Additionally, the obvious absorption peaks at 2937 and 1413 cm−1 in CQDs are associated with the stretching vibration C–H and C=C, respectively [50,51].

The peak at 1399 cm−1 in TiO2 is relevant to thelattice vibrationsof TiO2 [48]. The CQDs/TiO2 shows the new C-H peak appearing at 2930 cm−1 suggests the succesful coupling of TiO2 and CQDs. The characteristic peaks in the range from 400 to 800 cm−1 of TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 can be attributed to Ti-O-Ti stretching vibration in TiO2 [48,51,52].Compared with TiO2, the broad absorption band at 400–800 cm−1 in CQDs/TiO2 shows a red shift and becomes narrower due to the combination effect of Ti-O-Ti and Ti-O-C vibrations, which verifies the formation of Ti-O-C bond [48,52]. The peak at 1030 cm−1 in TiO2 is attributed to the vibration of Ti–O bond [53,54]. After coupling with CQDs, the peak becomes wider and shifts to higher wavenumber, also suggesting the formation of Ti-O-C bond [47,55]. The formation of Ti-O-C bond implies the strong interaction between TiO2 and CQDs, which is conducive to the interfacial charge transfer, thereby playing a significant role in the PEC properties of CQDs/TiO2 and CQDs/E-TiO2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Charge kinetics processes in PEC water oxidation

ce:section.ce:para:
To reveal the reasons of greatly increased photocurrent of TiO2 by electrochemical reduction and OV-rich CQDs modification, the charge separation and transfer behaviors of the photoanodes were first investigated by comparing their η bulk and η surface. To calculate the η bulk and η surface, the flux of the photogenerated holes reaching the electrode surface is estimated by measuring the photocurrent density of the photoanode with the use of Na2SO3 as hole scavenger. As shown in Fig. S15, the J-V curves measured in 0.2 M Na2SO4 containing 0.5, 1.0 and 1.5 M Na2SO3 have little change, suggesting the holes that reach the electrode surface can almost completely participate in the oxidation of Na2SO3. Therefore, the η surface and η bulk can be calculated by the eqs. (7) and (8), respectively [2–4]:

where J H2O (Fig. 2a) and J Na2SO3 (Fig. S16) are the photocurrent densities achieved by the oxidation of H2O and Na2SO3, respectively. The Jabs can be estimated by combining AM 1.5G solar spectrum (Fig. S17a) and light harvesting efficiency (LHE, Fig. S17b) according to the eqs. [2,4]:

where λ is the wavelength (nm); A(λ) is the absorbance at wavelength λ; λ e is the absorption edge wavelength of the photoanodes; N ph(λ) is the photon flux (mW cm−2 nm−1). The J abs values of TiO2, E-TiO2, CQDs/TiO2 and CQDs/E-TiO2 are 2.44, 2.51, 3.25 and 3.41 mA cm−2, respectively (Fig. S18).

The η bulk and η surface of the photoanodes are displayed in Fig. 5 a and Fig. 5b, respectively. The pristine TiO2 shows low η bulk and η surface. After electrochemical reduction, the η bulk and η surface of the obtained E-TiO2 are both improved, especially for the η bulk. The η bulk of E-TiO2 is sharply increased to 55.6% in comparison to that of TiO2 (4.48%) at 0.40 VRHE. The near-complete bulk charge separation is achieved for E-TiO2 at 1.23 VRHE (η bulk = 94.7%), which is 4.0 times higher than that of TiO2. The η surface of E-TiO2 is increased to 73.2% at 1.23 VRHE, which is 1.7 times higher than that of TiO2. The OV-rich CQDs modification has an ignorable effect on the η bulk for the obtained CQDs/TiO2 and CQDs/E-TiO2, with the η bulk values of 22.3% and 90.9% at 1.23 VRHE, respectively. However, the OV-rich CQDs modification can sharply improve the η surface of the samples, suggesting the role of OV-rich CQDs as OECs. The CQDs/E-TiO2 shows the η surface of 56.0% at 0.40 VRHE, which is 7.2 times higher than E-TiO2. The CQDs/E-TiO2 shows a high η surface of 82.5% at 1.23 VRHE. A higher η surface value at lower potential may account for the much lower onset potential of CQDs/E-TiO2 for PEC water oxidation. The simultaneous improvement of η bulk and η surface induced by electrochemical reduction of TiO2 and CQDs modification results in dramatically increased PEC activity of CQDs/E-TiO2.

As shown in Fig. S19, the electrochemical reduction time of 5–50 s has a negligible effect on the light harvesting edge, which suggests that the OV density induced by 5–50 s of electrochemical reduction almost don’t influence the J abs value. Therefore, the η surface and η bulk are determined by the J H2O and J Na2SO3 of the E-TiO2 photoanodes with different OV density. The photocurrent densities of the E-TiO2 photoanodes prepared with different reduction time for water oxidation or sulfite oxidation are shown in Fig. 5c, in which the E-TiO2 with 20 s of reduction both exhibits the highest photocurrent densities. The results imply that the OV density induced by 20 s of reduction is optimal for PEC water oxidation and bulk charge separation. The effect of Ov density on the η bulk and η surface of TiO2 is further studied (Fig. 5d). It indicates that the OV can simultaneously influence the η bulk and η surface of TiO2, and that the OV have a much bigger effect on the η bulk of TiO2. The OV densities with 20 s and 30 s of reduction time are optimal for the η bulk and η surface, respectively. The E-TiO2 with 20 s of reduction shows the best photocurrent density for water oxidation for the fact that the OV density induced by 20 s of reduction lead to the optimal comprehensive effect of η bulk and η surface.

To gain more insight into the electronic properties, the M-S plots of the samples were measured. The TiO2 shows a positive slope of the M-S curve, suggesting a n-type semiconductor for TiO2 (Fig. 6 a) [56]. The M-S curves of E-TiO2, CQDs/TiO2 and CQDs/E-TiO2 all show positive slopes, indicating that the semiconductor type of TiO2 is unaffectedby electrochemical reduction or/and CQDs modification. The flat band potential (V F) and charge carrier density (N D) can be obtained according to the eqs. (11) and (12), respectively [3]:
(11)

where C is the space charge capacitance; e is the fundamental electron charge (1.6 × 10−19C); ε is the relative permittivity of the semiconductor (35 for TiO2); ε0 is the permittivity of vacuum (8.86 × 10−12F/m); V is the applied potential. The V F (obtained from the intercept of × axis in Fig. 6a) of TiO2 is negatively shifted from −0.124 VRHE to −0.213 VRHE after electrochemical reduction, which is due to the reduction of the surface Fermi level pinning effect [28]. After modification of CQDs, the V F is negatively shifted to −0.147 VRHE for CQDs/TiO2 and −0.235 VRHE for CQDs/E-TiO2. The negative shift of V F suggests increased band bending of the composite photoanodes, which is beneficial for facilitating the charge transfer in the electrode/electrolyte interface, resulting in enhanced PEC performances [3]. As listed in Table S2, the N D of CQDs/E-TiO2 is 1.05 × 1020 cm−3, which is 16.6, 2.42 and 7.55 times higher than that of TiO2, E-TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2, respectively. The higher N D indicates enhanced electronic conductivity, resulting in better charge separation properties within the electrodes [27,28].

To further understand the interfacial charge transfer kinetics, the EIS measurements were applied under illumination. The EIS Nyquist plots of the samples are shown in Fig. 6b, in which only one semicircle is observed for each sample, suggesting the only one time constant [27]. Therefore, the EIS data can be fitted by an equivalent circuit model shown in the inset of Fig. 6b, where the R s and R ct represent the series resistance and interfacial charge transfer resistance, respectively [2,4,27,28]. The fitted results are listed in Table S2. The CQDs/E-TiO2 has the smallest R ct value of 349 Ω, compared with TiO2 (3364 Ω), E-TiO2 (768 Ω) and CQDs/TiO2 (1987 Ω), indicating the fastest charge transfer kinetics at the CQDs/E-TiO2/electrolyte interface [7].

The OER activities of the photoanodes can be estimated by comparing the J-V curves measured without irradiation (Fig. 6c). The onset potential of the photoanodes decreases in the order: TiO2 (2.69 VRHE) > E-TiO2 (2.60 VRHE) > CQDs/TiO2 (2.26 VRHE) > CQDs/E-TiO2 (2.06 VRHE), suggesting CQDs modification significantly accelerates the OER kinetics. To confirm the active sites of CQDs as the role of OECs, the relative Electrochemical surface areas (ECSA) of the TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 were determined from the capacitive region of cyclic voltammograms with the sweep rate of 10, 20, 30, 40, 50 and 60 mV/s, respectively (Fig. S20). It can be found that the CQDs/TiO2 is 1.36 times in surface area than the pristine TiO2, which may confirm abundant active sites of CQDs cocatalysts.

In addition, the photovoltage of the CQDs/E-TiO2 under illumination is 0.458 V, which is 306, 116 and 199 mV higher than that of TiO2, E-TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2, respectively (Fig. 6d). Under steady-state light irradiation, the gradient of quasi-Fermi level in the photoanode generates the photovoltage, which drive the photoinduced holes to be injected into the electrolyte [27]. The CQDs/E-TiO2 shows a higher photovoltage and thus a larger driving force for promoting charge separation, resulting in enhanced PEC activity.

The effects of electrochemical reduction and OV-rich CQDs modification on the charge transfer kinetics of TiO2 photoanode were characterized by the IMPS/IMVS [3,4]. The electron collection (transport) time (τ c) obtained from the IMPS curve represents the average time required for electrons transport from the photoanode to the FTO substrate. The electron recombination time or lifetime (τ r) extracted by the IMVS curve represents the lifetime of most charge carriers in the photoanode under open circuit condition. Fig. 7 a and Fig. 7b exhibit the IMPS and IMVS Nyquist plots of the samples, respectively. The τ c and τ r are calculated by the eqs. (13) and (14), respectively [3,4]:
(13)

where f min (IMPS) and f min (IMVS) are the frequency at the imaginary minimum in the IMPS and IMVS Nyquist plots, respectively. The CQDs/E-TiO2 shows a shorter τ c (30.2 ms) than TiO2 (116 ms), CQDs/TiO2 (81.5 ms) and E-TiO2 (41.6 ms), indicating the fastest electrons transport in CQDs/E-TiO2. Additionally, the CQDs/E-TiO2 shows a longer τ r (6.88 s) than TiO2 (0.916 s), CQDs/TiO2 (1.99 s) and E-TiO2 (4.51 s), indicating that the electron recombination in TiO2 photoanode is efficiently suppressed by creating OV and decorating OV-rich CQDs.

The competition between electron transfer and recombination is further studied by comparing the electron collection efficiency (η c) and electron average difussion length (L n) of the photoanodes, which are calculated by the eqs. (15) and (16), respectively [3,4]:

where d is the film thickness of the photoanodes. The η c value of CQDs/E-TiO2 is 99.6%, which is higher than that of TiO2 (87.3%), E-TiO2 (99.1%) and CQDs/TiO2 (95.9%) (Fig. 7c). The same trend can be found for the L n values, which are 4.95, 18.3, 8.70 and 26.6 μm for TiO2, E-TiO2, CQDs/TiO2 and CQDs/E-TiO2, respectively (Fig. 7d). The L n values of the photoanodes are much higher than the thicknesses (~2.7 μm), indicating that the electrons can be effectively collected by the conductive substrates. The higher η c and L n values for CQDs/E-TiO2 suggest the electrochemically induced OV in TiO2 and OV-rich CQDs modification greatly improve the charge transportproperty. The τ c, τ r, η c and L n of the photoanodes are listed in Table S3.

To further confirm the charge transferkinetics, the TPC and TPV were applied [3,4,57]. The TPC/TPV curves are fitted to a bi-exponential decay model: y = y0 + A1e−x/
τ
1 + A2e−x/
τ
2, and the average decay time (τ) is calculated by the equation: τ = (A1 τ 1 + A2 τ 2)/(A1 + A2). The TPC curve was measured under short circuit condition, which determines the time (τ t) needed for electron transport from the photoanode to the FTO substrate. The CQDs/E-TiO2 shows shorter τ t (1.21 ms) than TiO2 (3.42 ms), E-TiO2 (1.75 ms) and CQDs/TiO2 (2.86 ms) (Fig. 8 a). The electron lifetime (τ l) within the photoanode was extracted from the TPV curve measured under opencircuitcondition. As shown in Fig. 8b, the CQDs/E-TiO2 shows the longest τ l (3.83 s) in comparison to TiO2 (0.553 s), E-TiO2 (2.81 s) and CQDs/TiO2 (0.967 s). The TPC/TPV results agree well with the IMPS/IMVS data, implying that the synergy of the electrochemically induced OV in TiO2 and OV-rich CQDs greatly promotes the charge separation and transfer of CQDs/E-TiO2. The τ t and τ l of the photoanodes are listed in Table S4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of enhanced water oxidation performance
ce:section.ce:para: The valence band (VB) spectra of TiO2 and E-TiO2 were measured as shown in Fig. 9 a. The VB edge of TiO2 is about 2.34 eV below the Fermi level. The VB edge of E-TiO2 shows obvious blue-shift to 2.06 eV, accompanied by a band tail of 1.60 eV, which is ascribed to the presence of delocalized surface impurity states (shallow energy levels) originated from the electrochemical reduction induced OV [58,59]. Combined with the E g values, the CB minima of TiO2 and E-TiO2 are −0.65 and −0.92 eV, respectively. Furthermore, the OV could introduce localized states at 0.75–1.18 eV below the CB minimum of E-TiO2 [15,16]. The energy positions of localized states are calculated to be −0.17–0.26 eV below the Fermi level. Therefore, the energy band structures of TiO2 and E-TiO2 are shown in Fig. 9b. It was reported that the electron transitions from both tailed state and OV-induced localized states are responsible for the improved visible and near-infrared light absorption [15,16].

For n-type semiconductor, the CB locates at the position that is 0.1–0.3 eV higher than theVFB position [3,4]. Assuming potential difference between the CB andVFB is 0.1 eV, the CB potential of E-TiO2 is -0.313 eVRHE according to the V FB value of E-TiO2 (Fig. 6a). Therefore, the VB potential of E-TiO2 is 2.667 eVRHE. The absorption edge of CQDs locates at approximately 631.2 nm, giving the E g of 1.96 eV (Fig. 9c). The Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO) level of CQDs was determined by cyclicvoltammetry (CV), which was measured using the bare FTO and CQDs solution as the work electrode and electrolyte, respectively [2,4,60,61]. As shown in Fig. 9d, the LUMO level of CQDs is estimated to be − 0.37 VRHE. The HOMO level of CQDs is calculated to be 1.59 VRHE. Therefore, the type-II energy band alignment is confirmed for CQDs/ E-TiO2 heterojunction.

On the basis of the results above, the mechanism of PEC performance improvement for CQDs/E-TiO2 is proposed in Fig. 9e. A type-II heterojunction is formed for CQDs/E-TiO2, in which the photoinduced holes in the VB of E-TiO2 are injected into the HOMO level of CQDs for oxygen evolution, while the photoinduced electrons in the LUMO level of CQDs transfer to the CB of E-TiO2 and further reach the Pt electrode through an external circuit for hydrogen production. The CQDs/E-TiO2 heterojunction promotes the charge separation, leading to more holes reaching the photoanode surface. The OV in TiO2 can increase the majority carrier density and provide photoinduced charge traps for promoting bulk charge separation by serving as shallow donors [8,27,28]. However, excessive OV will in turn degrade the PEC performance of TiO2, because a large number of Ti3+ ions act as recombination centers of electron-hole pairs [28]. Meanwhile, the presence of OV can promote the surface OER processes, but the OV can also create surface trap states that lead to interfacial charge recombination [29]. Therefore, tuning the OV density in TiO2 is crucial to the water oxidation performance. The moderate OV in TiO2 induced by accuately electrochemical reduction result in the optimal comprehensive effect of bulk charge separation and surface charge transfer, which sharply improve the η bulk, acompanied by increased η surface.

Additionally, the OV-rich CQDs serve as the role of OECs due to the fact that the abundant OV in CQDs promote the hole transfer, which efficiently reduce the energy barrier of OER, thereby improving the η surface. Because CQDs can serve as visible light sensitizer due to the up-conversion effect, the CQDs/E-TiO2 shows improved visible light response in comparision to E-TiO2, which directly increases the J abs. The up-conversion PL of CQDs can be considered as an anti-stokes transition mechanism as illustrated in Fig. 9. Briefly, the low-energy photons excite the electron transition from π orbital to high-energy π* orbital, and than the electrons in π* orbital jump back to a lowr energy state (σ orbital), accompanied by the emission of up-converted PL [2,4,32,62,63]. It is the synergisticeffectof electrochemically induced OV in TiO2 and OV-rich CQDs modification that dramatically improves the PEC property (Fig. S21). Meanwhile, the excellent PEC performance of CQDs/E-TiO2 is highlighted by comparing our work with the reported works on CQDs modified TiO2 photoanodes (Table S5) and some recently reported TiO2-based photoanodes (Table S6) for PEC water oxidation or water splitting.

In conclusion, a novel CQDs/E-TiO2 photoanode is fabricated by a simple electrochemical reduction of TiO2 and further modification of OV-rich CQDs. Compared with the pristine TiO2, the E-TiO2 shows dramatically enhanced PEC performance due to the fact that the moderate OV accurately induced by electrochemical reduction sharply improve the η bulk. Compared with E-TiO2, the CQDs/E-TiO2 shows a further improved photocurrent density and remakably negative onset potential, because the OV-rich CQDs simultaneously serve as the roles of OECs and visible light sensitizers, significantly improving the η surface and J abs. This work sheds light on the synergisticalmechanismof electrochemically induced OV in TiO2 and OV-rich CQDs for improving the PEC performance, which may provide an efficient way of designing and fabricating excellent photoanodes for PEC water splitting.
